After the fall, they're experiencing pain and swelling over the radial aspect of the wrist.
That makes me think about the area around the radius bone, specifically the radial styloid process.
The radial styloid process is part of the radius, and it's usually located just below the ulnar styloid process.
In a normal wrist, the radial styloid is higher than the ulnar one, but here, the radial styloid is lower.
That's a key point because when the radial styloid is lower, it can cause tenderness in the anatomical snuff box, which is the area just below the radial styloid.
The anatomical snuff box is a small triangular area on the thumb side of the wrist.
If there's tenderness there, it's often a sign of a fracture, specifically a fracture of the radial styloid process.
This type of fracture is common in falls because the radial styloid is more susceptible to injury when the hand is outstretched.

So putting it all together, the patient's history of a fall, the location of the pain and swelling, the lower position of the radial styloid, and the tenderness in the snuff box all point towards a radial styloid fracture.
I don't think it's a Colles' fracture because that's a different type of wrist fracture, usually involving the distal radius.
The location and the specific tenderness in the snuff box are more indicative of the radial styloid process fracture.
